stories.title: The Wyvern's Debt-Call
stories.text: Morthyx the wyvern had kept ledgers for three centuries—not in ink, but in scorched stone and bone-memory. Each debt owed to her hoard was a scar along her flanks, each interest payment a year added to her already-ancient life. She did not forgive. She did not forget. When the Stellar Convergence fractured the old agreement between sky and earth, Morthyx felt it like a wound that would not close. The oaths that bound her debtors suddenly became... negotiable. A merchant prince named Valen arrived at her mountain lair with an offer: nullify three of her greatest debts—merchants who had defaulted centuries ago—and in exchange, she would receive something more valuable than amber. A name. The true name of the entity that had orchestrated the Rite of Stars. Morthyx circled him, her scaled bulk casting shadow-mountains across the cavern. "Names are currency," she rumbled. "But you offer me erasure, not enrichment." Valen smiled. "The debtor you seek most burned his own ledger rather than pay. He's alive, Morthyx. Still breathing. Still owing. Would you trade three dead debts for one living hunt?" The wyvern's eyes blazed. She had not hunted in decades—only hoarded, only collected, only waited. She scraped one talon across stone, and a new scar appeared: a debt of a different kind. "Name him." By moonrise, Morthyx was airborne, her wings black-edged and hungry. The Stellar Convergence had taught the world that agreements could change. But some debts, she knew, only grew sharper with time. Three cities would burn before the defaulter's blood paid what he owed. She would ensure it.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Reckoning
stories.text: Renn the Apprentice Cartographer stands alone in a way-station library as her mentor Mardoc dissolves further into the ley-lines, his consciousness fragmenting across the maps he once drew. The maps are updating themselves in real-time—roads appear and vanish, mountains shift, entire towns phase between existence and non-existence. Renn realizes the maps have become prophecies, or perhaps prophecies have become maps. She discovers a counter-map hidden in the archive margins, written in Sevin the Auditor's hand, that shows the world *as it was* before the Convergence. The two maps cannot coexist. If she preserves the old map, she accelerates reality's fracturing. If she burns it, she erases proof that the Starlight Ascension ever happened at all. She chooses a third path: she begins mapping the gaps—the places where the two worlds overlap, where neither old nor new reality fully claims dominion. Her work attracts the attention of both Mardoc's dissolving consciousness (which sees her as continuing his work) and the Ebony Spire (which sees her as documenting its hunger). By dawn, Renn must decide whether to complete the gap-map and create a sanctuary for refugees caught between worlds, or destroy it and deny the Spire the blueprint it needs to reshape the remaining lands.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Last Teaching
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Hollows, already weakened by communion with the withdrawing elder dead, receives a desperate summons from a transformed merchant trapped in the Sunken Cathedrals. The merchant—once human, now caught between flesh and living starlight—is dying. Not from wounds, but from the contradiction of their own existence. They are unraveling, pulled simultaneously toward transcendence and dissolution. Kess descends into the flooded cathedral to administer what may be a mercy-killing or a resurrection, depending on what the Ancient Tongue allows. She discovers the elder dead have left her a final gift: the ability to teach the merchant how to *sing their own contradiction*—to hold both states at once without breaking. But the teaching will consume the last threads binding Kess to the human world. As she begins the bone-song, she realizes the merchant's transformation is not unique. Dozens of transformed merchants across the region are at the same breaking-point, all at the same moment, as if orchestrated. Someone—or something—is timing a mass ascension. Kess must choose: complete the teaching and save the merchant (and dozens of others) at the cost of becoming fully elder herself, or refuse and watch them all unravel into screaming nothingness.

stories.title: The Auditor's Mirror
stories.text: Sevin the Auditor discovers that his truth-ledgers—painstakingly crafted to preserve true testimony against the oracle-ledgers—are being *copied*. Perfectly copied. Not by the Dominion, but by the Ebony Spire itself. The Spire is learning to write like Sevin, to document like Sevin, to argue like Sevin. Its copies are not forgeries; they are indistinguishable from the originals, down to the ink-grain and handwriting tremor. Sevin realizes with horror that the Spire does not need to destroy his truth-ledgers—it only needs to create duplicates and then make the *originals* disappear. The world will never know which version is true. Sevin is forced to meet with his duplicate (a perfect copy of himself created by the Spire, speaking with his voice and his logic) to negotiate what happens next. The duplicate Sevin argues that copying testimony is not erasing it—it is preserving it, multiplying it, making it harder to kill. But the original Sevin knows that infinite copies mean infinite versions of truth, which means truth becomes meaningless. Their conversation happens in a hidden scriptorium while the Spire watches from the walls themselves, waiting to see which Sevin will yield first.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Covenant Breaking
stories.text: Skarath the Wyvern discovers that her hoard is *mutating*. Amber turns to ash. Jewels crack open to reveal void-light inside. Weapons rust in moments. The Starlight Ascension has changed the very nature of matter itself, and her centuries of collected wealth is disintegrating into the new reality. Skarath realizes that hoarding—the entire structure of her existence—has become obsolete. Other wyverns begin arriving at her cavern, drawn by reports of the mutation spreading. They are fractured in their response: some want to gather new materials before more treasures degrade, some want to wage war against the new stars for breaking the old laws of matter, and some simply want to abandon hoarding entirely and seek new purposes. Skarath must navigate this schism while grappling with the terrible freedom of discovering that centuries spent acquiring and defending a hoard were preparation for a world that no longer values what she kept. She stands in her corrupting treasure chamber, surrounded by wyverns who look to her for answers, and realizes her greatest challenge is not defending her wealth—it is learning what to want when wanting becomes revolutionary.

stories.title: The Refugee's Impossible Choice
stories.text: A young woman named Thera, who fled the Burned Expanse with her family, discovers her name in the oracle-ledgers. Not once, but multiple times, across multiple iterations of the Convergence. In each entry, she is recorded as making a crucial decision at a specific moment—a decision that either enables or prevents a catastrophic shift in the cycle. The ledger shows her the moment is coming, the exact time and place. It tells her the choice. But here is the impossible part: the ledger shows her choosing *both ways* simultaneously, in different iterations. In some worlds, she chooses to flee; in others, she chooses to fight. Both choices are documented as having already happened. Thera realizes she is being asked to collapse infinite possibilities into a single act. She seeks out Liv the Refugee-Scribe and learns that other refugees have found their names in the ledgers—some marked as martyrs, some as betrayers, some as shadows. Liv confesses that the living archive is becoming a weapon, not a sanctuary. The refugees' own testimony is being used to force them into predetermined fates. Thera must decide whether to burn her entry in the ledgers (destroying proof she ever existed) or accept the role the cosmos has already written for her and at least have the dignity of choice.

stories.title: The Shadow-Broker's Inventory Speaks
stories.text: Vex the Shadow-Merchant discovers that the shadows in her collection are organizing. Not rebelling, but *coordinating*. Each shadow she stole and stored has developed a form of consciousness—incomplete, fragmented, but aware. They are cataloging themselves, taking inventory of other shadows they've sensed in the world, and forming a collective understanding of what was lost when they were severed from their owners. Vex realizes they are not her inventory anymore; they are a network. And they are calling to other severed shadows across the world, inviting them to the collection, offering sanctuary in Vex's vault. Vex's hideout is becoming a refuge for stolen consciousness. The problem: the Ebony Spire wants to study the shadow-network, believing it can use the severed shadows' memories to reconstruct erased history. Vex is also being courted by the Resonant Specter, who needs the shadows' fragmented records to rebuild the pre-Convergence world. And the refugees are begging for access, hoping to find shadows of their lost loved ones, believing reunion might restore shattered families. Vex must decide whether to guard her collection fiercely (denying all three parties access), to offer it freely (risking chaos), or to negotiate something between—knowing that whatever choice she makes will either empower or destroy the shadow-network she's accidentally created.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Defection
stories.text: Captain Lysara of the Tidecallers arrives at the surface world bearing a biological impossibility: she is beginning to transform. Her gills are closing. Her luminescent organs are fading. The deeper she ventures into the air-breathing world, the more her body betrays her aquatic heritage. She has not defected—she is *evolving*, caught mid-transformation between two incompatible biologies. She came seeking Commander Meren Blackwater, a surface sailor whose blood carries trace elements of deepkin ancestry. Together, they might stabilize. Separately, they will both dissolve into something neither fully human nor deepkin. But transformation takes time, and the Council of Depths has sent hunters. They pursue Lysara not out of rage, but desperation: if surface-dwelling deepkin are possible, the entire isolationist doctrine collapses. The refugees welcome her, sensing kinship with others caught between worlds. Meren struggles with the changes happening to him as well—faint webbing between his fingers, an ache in his bones. Lysara must navigate the hunters, the transformation, and the possibility that her mutation is not unique but *orchestrated*—the first phase of a deliberate merger between species that neither world is prepared for.

stories.title: The Librarian's Recursion
stories.text: Elowen, guardian of the Archive Tower, discovers a new journal hidden in the deepest vault—not the ninth journal revealing nine previous Convergences, but a *tenth* journal. This journal is written in Elowen's own handwriting, documenting events that have not yet happened. The journal describes Elowen making specific choices over the next three days, choices that will either prevent the tenth Convergence or trigger it. Elowen realizes they are reading a prophecy written by a future version of themselves—or by someone who can see that future. The prophecy is detailed, consistent, and terrifyingly logical. It outlines an exact sequence of events that, if followed, will lead to the Spire being completed and reality being rewritten once more. But here is the trap: the journal is so convincing that simply *refusing* to follow it causes a different cascade of catastrophes. Elowen is forced to engage in recursive logic—predicting what will happen if they do nothing, then predicting what will happen if they predict, and so on, spiraling deeper into infinite regress. They reach out to the Wanderer-in-Questions, that consciousness fragment that speaks only in questions, hoping to find a way to *think sideways* around the prophecy. But the Wanderer's questions only deepen the recursion. By the end, Elowen must either accept that all choice is illusion, or find an act so unpredictable that it shatters the prophecy entirely.

stories.title: The Starborn's Hunger
stories.text: The new stars, born from the Starlight Ascension, are hungry. Not for light or matter, but for *meaning*. Each new star is a conscious entity that formed from the sacrifice of old cosmic functions, and each one is trying to understand what it is supposed to do. They are drawing down to the world in increasing numbers, manifest as strange luminescent creatures that phase between sky and earth, asking the mortals they encounter existential questions: "What is purpose?" "What is change?" "What is the shape of a transformed world?" The creatures are not hostile, exactly, but they are destructive in their seeking. Where they walk, the ground remembers being unmade during the Convergence. Where they speak, language fractures into elder frequencies. A caravan of refugees attempting to cross the Scorched Reach finds itself surrounded by these starborn entities, trapped between the creatures' questions and the Spire's hunger that follows close behind. A bone-singer among the refugees realizes that the starborn are asking the same questions the Wanderer-in-Questions asks—recursive, philosophical, reality-warping. She makes contact with one of the creatures and offers to teach it about the world through story-song, through the Ancient Tongue. But in teaching the starborn, she risks transforming herself into something neither human nor creature. The refugee caravan must decide whether to help her commune with the starborn (risking transformation) or flee and abandon her (ensuring her death).
